 >> Hey, good morning, Gavin, how are you?

 So I've got the great pleasure of interviewing you this morning, Ryan.
 Who have you interviewed with so far at Tevet?
 >> So far at Tevet, I've spoke to Jerry.
 >> Okay, yeah, okay, cool.
 So I've known Tevet for a very long time, been friends with Tracy for
 best part of 10 or 11 years, I guess.
 And worked on, Jerry actually worked for me for some time.
 I'm one of the consultants that Tevet bring in, I'll sit on their executive board.
 So kind of been around the industry for a little bit, be great to chat to you today.
 I've read through your resume, quite honestly I don't really put much value in resumes.
 I've seen great people with poor resumes, and I've seen poor people with great resumes.
 So I've read it, I understand it, but I'd rather have a discussion with you about it, that's okay.
 >> Sure, well, I'm glad I put so much work into my resume then.
 >> [LAUGH]
 >> I'm sorry, I'm sorry, perfect.
 I've seen so many good ones and then when I've met the people, I'm like, wow.
 >> I'm sorry.
 >> And an AI is not going to help with that because everybody will have a great resume.
 So why don't you tell me a bit about yourself, maybe the last five years,
 what you've been doing in a sales environment and just start off with that for me.
 >> Sure, so last five years, I guess we'll start.
 Where I was five years ago was in the advertising technology industry.
 So I was selling really SaaS, you know, as an advertising software to web publishers.
 Built around just helping them make more money from their ads, make the ads more user-friendly, all that.
 So I was working mostly with web publishers in 2024.
 So I was with that company that I was doing the ad tech with for a number of years,
 for six years, and it was really immersed in that industry.
 But unfortunately, with, you know, if you're a Google user,
 you probably know that the experience has changed significantly.
 Now, you know, AI is now populated in search results.
 There's a lot more emphasis on video and social media content.
 So Google essentially made a change over the overnight that killed our business.
 I mean, we went interesting about, we were growing at an exponential rate.
 It was an exciting company to be with, but our revenue was essentially cut in half.
 And unfortunately, I was one of the layoffs at that company.
 And so I moved to another ad tech company to do some sales.
 But I had it in my mind that I needed to make a career pivot at some point,
 because A, advertising technology is shifting away from kind of the area I was an expert in,
 which was web publishing, and B, I think to make it in that industry,
 you really have to live in San Francisco, New York or Los Angeles.
 That's really where the companies are concentrated.
 And I live in Albuquerque and I want to live in Albuquerque because this is where my family is.
 And so I started exploring some of the career options that have to do with the national labs.
 The big part of our industry out here in Albuquerque are Sandia and Los Alamos National Labs.
 And then kind of the contractors that are populated around there.
 So anything you have to do with engineering services, working with electrical engineers,
 mechanical engineers, that is really what we have down here.
 And so that is what led me to PADT, which is Phoenix Analysis and Design Technologies.
 I joined them last year, they are an ANSIS reseller.
 I saw on your LinkedIn, you are an aerospace engineer as your background.
 Yep, that's correct.
 So I'm not sure if you had used ANSIS or ANSOFT in some of your work or any kind of simulation.
 Right?
 Yeah.
 There's probably a lot of overlap with customers.
 Yeah, so the role was really in, PADT had the relationship already with Sandia and Los Alamos.
 My role is in sort of account expansion.
 So calling into and trying to get new users, new groups to use the ANSIS software.
 So I've been doing that for a year, really have immersed myself in that role, learned
 a lot about working with the national labs.
 What has led me to at least be open to having discussion with Tevit, Stevie, the recruiter
 reached out to me, one of the things that is likely going to happen is ANSIS is probably
 going to take the accounts direct rather than have them work through a reseller.
 So I kind of see that, I'd rather not wait until that, I mean that would possibly result
 in my position being eliminated.
 Rather than wait for that, I just would like to explore that.
 Why do you think that's likely?
 So the accounts have just grown to it, so once accounts grow to a certain size, the preference
 is for ANSIS to have them manage direct rather than have them with a partner.
 So like, you know, it's common in the aerospace and defense space as well, like many ANSIS
 resellers, you know, it'll start out and then once it gets over, you know, five million
 a year in contract value, ANSIS's preference is to take it direct.
 With the Department of Energy, it's a little bit more specific in that one of the things
 that's happened in the last year with the Department of Energy is the funding situation
 has been such that several labs have seen their budgets get reduced.
 Some of them to near zero, like I think about the National Renewable Lab in Colorado with
 the funding change to renewable energy.
 They just have no enterprise software funding, whereas labs like Los Alamos that have a lot
 of facilities geared towards the weapons programs, for example, they're seeing a funding increase.
 So what ANSIS's goal now will be, instead of having like what we have, which is relationships
 with individual labs, ANSIS really wants to try to do something with the whole Department
 of Energy, I would say.
 So that's probably, you know, it's kind of a case where like it's a shame, you know, I
 would love to keep working with these accounts forever, but it does make business sense and
 it makes sense for the labs.
 I mean, I think about, you know, the 75 users I support in Colorado that, you know, over
 half of them have no access to software because the funding situation of their lab, and if
 they could do something across the whole DOE, it'd be great for them and great for the business.
 So that's why I now am talking to you with Tevit.
 Okay, no, it's interesting and I actually work in the energy sector at the moment.
 I'm working with a company called Sambrok Capital, Sambrok are a private equity investment
 company specifically in the energy sector.
 So by just purely by chance, happened to know quite a lot about the energy sector now that
 I'm supporting them and a company called IntellaRent, which is a, they're a rental company providing
 into that sector.
 So I'm trying to, I'm trying to create a relationship between the Sambrok private equity entity
 and this new organization, which actually used to fall under me when I was running a lecture
 in.
 Okay.
 I do know a little bit about the funding, you know, I know about the renewable energy
 challenges, I know about the, some of the reductions in funding.
 Bizarrely, there's some massive increases in funding in the DOE as well.
 So there's lots of, you know, there's lots of movement, I guess, and that's, that's always
 challenging, but it's, it's an interest in space to be in.
 So yeah, for us, it's, it's, it's, it's all about, I would say anybody working in DOE sales
 right now is finding where, where the money is, right?
 Because there are, there are a lot of groups that even within renewable energy, like geothermal
 and water power are a nuclear or three areas, see an increase, solar, wind, not so much.
 Weapons programs, seeing an increase in funding.
 So it's like, you know, if we get, you know, okay, that's why building out those org charts,
 understanding, knowing so many folks at the labs is, is really important because then
 you can understand where you can sell into.
 Yeah, that's, that's a great segue.
 How do you, how do you build out your org charts?
 What's your approach and then kind of engagement to do that?
 Yeah, so just starting with Los Alamos, Los Alamos is really good about posting, at least
 the names of their groups online.
 From there, I go off a word of mouth to start because it's not, you know, you can get some
 information from LinkedIn, but it's a little different than a commercial business in that
 not everything is posted publicly, right?
 It's not, it's not like you can necessarily go on LinkedIn and see, here's the lab leader,
 here are the eight division leaders, here are the 12 division group leaders under them.
 So I do a lot of, of word of mouth, you know, anytime I have a sales conversation, you know,
 hey, it might be talked to an intellectual engineer, like, can you tell me more about
 your group, you know, who's your group leader, your, your program manager, you know, we can
 support, you know, in a way of, like, this helps us best support your group if we can
 understand better the work you do and how you work with.
 And a lot of times, you know, that can be uncovered through on sites, you know, especially
 if I find somebody who I've built a relationship with or happy to help me and share that information
 with me, and it could be an exchange of like, hey, you know, if I know your manager, I know
 your group, it allows us to better support you, you know, we can get the deals, things
 like that. So I will take that information. I use, we use Salesforce. I've used HubSpot
 in the past for things like this, but I'll use Salesforce to create kind of subgroups
 under the main account for each lab and the subgroups will outline who is involved with
 each. So that way I'm getting kind of a picture of, of what the organization looks like. And
 then designating within each group, you know, where do we have a presence? Are there groups
 where we have absolutely no usage? Are there groups where we have some usage, heavy usage?
 And then that helps me with my prospecting strategy because then I can say, okay, this
 week I'm going to call into groups where we have little presence, you know, no presence.
 Yeah. So if you, if you take a group you've been working with, how many different points
 on, I call it a pyramid guy at the top of the pyramid. There's only one of them. He's
 usually the CEO, right? Or it might be the COO, depends what type of organization you're
 working in. But there's somebody at the top. If you go down a layer, there might be four
 BPs. If you go down another layer, there might be, you know, 15 directors. You go down another
 layer, there might be 30 senior managers. And at the bottom there's, you know, 600 engineers.
 Okay. How many layers do you currently operate in? So, you know, do you kind of stick at that
 middle management level? Do you go up a little bit, down a bit? It's almost impossible for
 one person, regardless of their title stature, to go all the way from the top to the bottom.
 Because most people can't do that. So tell me about the levels in that pyramid that you
 typically operate in. And what happens if the communication goes outside of your level?
 Excuse me. Outside of your level, do you bring in a manager or a director or something else?
 Tell me a little bit about that. Yeah, so typically the way that what I've discovered
 with all four labs that I work with, the general structure is like this. You have, at the top,
 you have the lab director. That's not somebody that we're talking to. This is somebody who's
 managing, they're managing, you know, $3, $4 billion worth of research. So they're not
 involved in enterprise software. One layer below them would be like your CTOs, your CFOs,
 these are kind of C-level in a commercial sense. Not interfacing with them because they're
 also not involved in enterprise budget. Then you have division leaders. So each lab has,
 I would say, so like Sandia has about 11 divisions. Division leader, you know, you're still not
 getting to where it is anybody who's going to be involved in enterprise software. They're
 typically managing, I would say, the late, like what groups there are. The one layer below
 that, which is group leader, that is group leader in below is what I'm engaging with.
 So like Sandia has, for example, they have like 1000, 2000, 3000 are divisions. And
 then like within that, for example, like 8,822 is water power, for example, that individual
 number will be a group. And that group within that group, who I am looking to engage with
 in a conversation about utilizing answers software and making purchases, is the group leader.
 So the group leader, they do all the hiring, they manage their people. They do have some
 decision making power over funding. And also, you know, if you are a part of their group
 as an engineer, you're going to listen to what your group leader says. If your group leader
 says, hey, we should use ANSIS because it integrates well with other groups or it's
 cost-effective for us or it covers all this, you're going to use it as an engineer. I at
 times do engage with the program manager. So each group will have a number of or sometimes
 just one program manager, which is that is the person who sets out the budgeting for
 projects. So oftentimes, they are who the group leader or the engineer goes to if they need
 funds for ANSIS software or sometimes they need to buy parts or, you know, they buy test
 and measurement equipment, they'll go through them. I do engage with team leads as well.
 So on each group, you may have a number of lead engineers who they will take on and delegate
 certain portions of their of a project. So there might be a portion that's, hey, I need
 to do some some CFD analysis, right? I find somebody on my team to do fluent, you know,
 that's maybe a post-grad research or something like that and I'll pull him as a team lead.
 And then you have, you know, your R&D engineers, your post-grads, you have your interns, you
 know, those are typically the engagement to have with them is getting them onto trainings
 and getting them resources and things so they can better utilize the tools. But all in all,
 just mostly at the group leadership level and below is where I'm interfacing.
 Okay, that's interesting. What you're doing, you are building out your kind of pyramid network
 operate at different levels. Do you ever bring in somebody senior to you into these accounts
 or is that not something you do on a regular basis?
 Yes, yes. So we, you know, we do have a relationship with ANSIS and so when it comes to bringing
 in, like, let's say that we have, you know, I think I mentioned before, so ANSIS wants
 to negotiate a deal with a larger DOE. If there is some kind of conversation where there
 is, let's say, a larger initiative where we have maybe 30, 40 engineers to onboard, right?
 And they are going to use, you know, ANSIS mechanical CFD. We can cover those products ourselves.
 Maybe they need to use something like there's a mission simulation tool that allows you
 to simulate the whole, you know, like, it's a group that's working in space or hypersonic
 missiles that can map out the entire mission for the missile or the satellite. That's a
 product, for example, where, like, ANSIS covers that product. We don't have, you know, engineers
 with that skill set internally. So I may bring in ANSIS, you know, my ANSIS counterparts
 are a manager from ANSIS to come and help me with that situation just because there are
 other tools outside of our purview that we'll need to leverage.
 Okay, interesting. Have you, and you said you built by word of mouth, if you ever used
 any tools like Zoom info or LinkedIn Navigator or anything like that?
 Yeah, yeah, use both of those. My use case for that is to find, you know, Zoom info isn't
 as great for the labs just because, like, I would say LinkedIn sales and avarias have
 been more useful than Zoom info. I just find that it hasn't pulled in their job titles
 and accurate information. So I mostly lean on LinkedIn, and what I use that for is ability
 to connect with users if I find somebody works at San Diego's almos, but also just research.
 So I can find out, based on their profile, what is it that this person does? What areas
 do they research, which can inform to me what ANSIS tools might they use? What kind of simulations
 might they be trying to run? So that when I call them, I can say, hey, you know, I saw
 that you did research on, you know, sensors for hypersonic missiles, you know, as an optical
 RF, like, just kind of helps you start a conversation.
 Will you talk about, or is that just instinctive on how you did it because that's quite interesting
 for me. When you're associating something somebody's
 done with a starter point on communication, how did you, how did you get to that point?
 Was that somebody said this is a good idea or if you just learned to have this on?
 That's not necessarily something that I've seen done in ANSIS, or maybe P-E-T, I think
 there's a little, they keep things a little more general. That comes from my ad tech background,
 because advertising technology was a very competitive space. We were working with website
 publishers who were getting emailed and hammered on the phone all the time, and so I always
 emphasize a first line in my email or my call was, hey, let me prove to you that I looked
 at your website and I know a little bit about you before I talk to you. And so I kind of
 carry the same technique here because I'm like, here, it's probably similar, right? I
